A language independent method for Named Entity Recognition will outperform comparable approaches on standard datasets and enhance monolingual NER systems.

Pretrained language models, specifically BERT, can be used for sequential sentence classification to capture contextual dependencies without the need for hierarchical encoding or Conditional Random Fields.
Automatically generated training data for lexical normalization can achieve performance close to that of manually annotated data.
It is possible to recover arbitrary sentences nearly perfectly with language models and representations of moderate size.
Passage retrieval is feasible in the language modeling context and can provide more reliable performance than retrieval based on full documents.
Communication-based evaluations will better align with quality categories of natural language generation than n-gram overlap methods.
A simple BERT-based model can achieve state-of-the-art performance for relation extraction and semantic role labeling without using any external features.
It is possible to generate token-level explanations for Natural Language Inference without the need for training data explicitly annotated for this purpose.
Zero-shot learning will enable the extraction of entities specified by a natural language query from a single web page without relying on seed examples.
The novel language modeling objective provided consistent performance improvements on every benchmark, without requiring any additional annotated or unannotated data.
The neural framework developed in this study will effectively empower the sequence labeling task by extracting knowledge from raw texts without relying on additional supervision.
Visual n-gram models can predict arbitrary phrases relevant to the content of an image without using manually labeled images.
AspMem will outperform state-of-the-art methods in opinion summarization and aspect identification tasks without relying on human supervision.
Automatic techniques developed for estimating the valence from relatively small corpora are more efficient if the corpora used contain texts similar to the one that must be evaluated.

A linguistically-based approach to answering non-factoid open-domain questions from unstructured data will achieve near-to-human-performance results and be competitive with state-of-the-art systems on SQuAD and MS-MARCO datasets.
The multi-task model outperforms previous models in scientific information extraction without using any domain-specific features.
The parser bypasses the need for part-of-speech tagging while using word embeddings based on universal tag distributions.
Unsupervised classification is highly accurate for distinguishing between original and translated texts.
HyTER provides better estimates of machine and human translation accuracy than alternative evaluation metrics.
The unsupervised approach to sentence boundary detection achieves good results without any further amendments or language-specific resources.
The introduced neural architectures will obtain state-of-the-art performance in named entity recognition without relying on language-specific knowledge or resources.
The proposed method without using manually designed features can achieve better performance on recognizing the discourse level relations in all of the relations.
BERTScore correlates better with human judgments and provides stronger model selection performance than existing metrics.
Using a large language model for paraphrasing can generate paraphrases at both sentence and paragraph levels without breaking the text into smaller chunks.

The proposed method exploits structured parsing information to detect claims without resorting to contextual information, achieving performance comparable to state-of-the-art methods that heavily rely on the context.
An unsupervised model can accurately estimate the per-instance reliability of each annotator and the correct label for each instance in natural language labeling tasks.
The neural and phrase-based models will outperform existing methods in machine translation without using parallel sentences.
The practical system for predicting sentence specificity outperforms the state-of-the-art method for predicting sentence specificity and does not require part of speech tagging or syntactic parsing.
An unsupervised approach to lexical simplification using regular corpora is as effective as systems that rely on simplified corpora.
Machine translation without parallel training data can be effectively approached as a decipherment task.
The novel model architecture will outperform standard models when training on monolingual text without transliteration and testing on code-switched text with alternate scripts.
Social dialogue systems can be trained effectively from raw unannotated data.

given_idea: The proposed approach will enable neural models to learn more accurate attention patterns, leading to substantial improvements in sentiment classification accuracy.
original: Progressive self-supervised attention learning approach for neural aspect-level sentiment classification models will yield better attention mechanisms, leading to substantial improvements over state-of-the-art models.

top_k.sentence: An attention model will significantly improve the accuracy of target-dependent sentiment analysis by modeling the contribution of each word with respect to the target.

Jointly learning discourse parsing and sentiment analysis using neural models will result in improvements in both tasks, with Multi-tasking having a bigger impact than Pre-training.

top_k.sentence: Simple data augmentation techniques can boost performance for named entity recognition models, especially for small training sets.

Using non-speaker annotations for building low-resource Named Entity Recognition models produces results that are consistently on par or better than cross-lingual methods built on modern contextual representations.

The multilingual data augmentation method will enable better generalization of Named Entity Recognition models for low-resource languages through the use of both language-specific and language-independent features.
A simple demonstration-based learning method for named entity recognition will significantly improve model performance in low-resource settings.
Maximum entropy models will improve the accuracy of named entity recognition compared to baseline methods.
Document-level optimization approach to named entity recognition will improve the effectiveness of domain-specific named entity recognition tasks compared to traditional sentence-level optimization.
Joint learning of named entity recognition and entity linking improves performance in both tasks compared to models trained with individual objectives.
Lexicon infused phrase embeddings will improve named entity recognition performance compared to existing methods.
Embedding words and entity types into a low-dimensional vector space will yield substantial improvements in Named-Entity Recognition performance.

The new data augmentation method for low-resource named entity recognition will outperform previous methods without data augmentation by over 40% in F1 and prior best data augmentation methods by over 2.0% in F1.
The weak supervision approach will improve Named Entity Recognition performance in the absence of labelled data.
Leveraging document-level features significantly improves named entity recognition quality, and fine-tuning generally outperforms feature-based approaches.
Deep affix features will improve the performance of neural named entity recognizers in multilingual and multi-domain settings.
The two-step rationale-centric data augmentation method significantly improves the performance of cross-domain Named Entity Recognition tasks compared to previous state-of-the-art methods.
Utilizing global structured information from dependency trees will improve the performance of named entity recognition models.
Enhancing Named Entity Recognition models with correlated samples will improve their efficacy on texts from special domains.
The end-to-end approach for named entity recognition from English speech outperforms the classical two-step approach.
An extension based on prototypical networks improves performance in low-resource named entity recognition tasks.
Incorporating named entity recognition into neural machine translation will improve named entity translation.
A language independent method for Named Entity Recognition will outperform comparable approaches on standard datasets and enhance monolingual NER systems.

The proposed method achieves state-of-the-art or competitive named entity recognition performance on commonly tested languages under a cross-lingual setting with much lower resource requirements than past approaches.
Jointly training named entity recognition and word segmentation with a long short-term memory conditional random field model yields nearly 5% absolute improvement over previously published results.
The lightweight multilingual named entity recognition and linking system achieves state-of-the-art performance on multilingual data and English AIDA CONLL data.
A multi-task approach employing Named Entity segmentation will improve Named Entity Recognition in social media data compared to traditional methods.
Using lexicalized constituency trees to model nested entities will enhance the performance of nested named entity recognition.
Adding context as additional sentences to BERT input systematically increases named entity recognition performance.
Coreference-aware word representations will improve the performance of neural named entity recognition compared to traditional models that ignore coreference relations.

Local Additivity based Data Augmentation (LADA) method for semi-supervised Named Entity Recognition (NER) will improve both entity and context learning by creating virtual samples through interpolation.
Using temporal information in named entity recognition models will improve their performance compared to models that disregard temporal information.
Using named entity information for language model adaptation significantly improves performance compared to a baseline generic language model.
Neural character embeddings will significantly improve named entity recognition performance compared to handcrafted features.
The method based on Weakly Supervised Latent Dirichlet Allocation can accurately perform Named Entity Recognition in Query and outperform the baseline methods.
Including manual features for part-of-speech, word shapes, and gazetteers can improve the performance of a neural CRF model in named entity recognition.
Dependency-guided LSTM-CRF model will improve named entity recognition by capturing long-distance dependencies and syntactic relationships in dependency trees.
A two-stage approach to handle non-local dependencies in Named Entity Recognition will outperform existing approaches while being more computationally efficient.
Zero-shot entity typing approach will outperform state-of-the-art supervised named entity recognition systems on out-of-training datasets.
The proposed methods outperform the nested named entity recognition state of the art on four corpora: ACE-2004, ACE-2005, GENIA and Czech CNEC.
Negative sampling improves named entity recognition performance through low missampling rate and high uncertainty.
Using external speech and/or text data that are not annotated for the task improves performance in low-resource spoken named entity recognition.
Improved differentiable architecture search will outperform strong baselines in named entity recognition tasks.
Adding named-entity feature with named-entity recognition into the source language produces better translation with neural machine translation.
Leveraging different types of syntactic information through attentive ensemble will improve named entity recognition performance.
MINER improves out-of-vocabulary named entity recognition performance through mutual information based training objectives.
A noise-robust learning scheme will improve the training of named entity recognition models using distantly-labeled data.
The bootstrapped iterative Named Entity Recognition model integrating Positive Unlabeled learning will effectively recognize named entities in a low-resource setting.
Methods fusing external knowledge into transformer models will achieve better performance in fine-grained multilingual named entity recognition compared to traditional methods.
Knowledge-Augmented Language Model (KALM) learns to recognize named entities in an entirely unsupervised way by using entity type information latent in the model.
Automatic pre-annotation helps improve human annotation speed and accuracy in out-of-domain named entity annotation.
Unsupervised consistency training using paraphrasing will improve model predictions for Named Entity Recognition when annotations are limited.
Stacking and voting methods for combining strong classifiers will achieve error rate reductions on named-entity recognition tasks compared to standard baselines.

given_idea: Exclusive Supermask Subnetwork Training (ExSSNeT) will improve performance while preventing forgetting in Continual Learning compared to existing methods.
original: NA

top_k.sentence: The proposed method will alleviate catastrophic forgetting in continual learning with deep neural networks.
Task-conditioned hypernetworks will significantly reduce catastrophic forgetting in continual learning by maintaining task-specific weight realizations.
The Supermasks in Superposition model can sequentially learn thousands of tasks without catastrophic forgetting.
Sparse neural Network for Continual Learning (SNCL) achieves state-of-the-art result for mitigating forgetting.
Continual learning will enable artificial neural networks to accumulate knowledge over different tasks without catastrophic forgetting.
Supervised Contrastive Replay (SCR) substantially reduces catastrophic forgetting and outperforms state-of-the-art continual learning methods by a significant margin on a variety of datasets.
The proposed continual learning framework for neural machine translation models will alleviate the problem of catastrophic forgetting and improve performance compared to baseline models.
A dual-memory self-organizing architecture will significantly outperform current methods of lifelong learning in incremental learning scenarios.
The mechanisms that mitigate catastrophic forgetting in neural networks vary based on the incremental training paradigm and type of data being used.
The Adaptive Knowledge Accumulation framework alleviates catastrophic forgetting on seen domains and demonstrates the ability to generalize to unseen domains.
The lifelong learning solution using autoencoders will reduce catastrophic forgetting in vision systems compared to state-of-the-art methods.
Variational continual learning (VCL) outperforms state-of-the-art continual learning methods on a variety of tasks, avoiding catastrophic forgetting in a fully automatic way.
Adding multiple tasks to a single deep neural network while avoiding catastrophic forgetting will result in better robustness against catastrophic forgetting compared to prior work.

ConTinTin (Continual Learning from Task Instructions) will enable a system to learn a sequence of new tasks one by one from textual instructions while improving forward-transfer and backward-transfer.
Intelligent synapses will dramatically reduce forgetting while maintaining computational efficiency in continual learning of classification tasks.
The less-forgetting learning method is effective in alleviating the catastrophic forgetting problem in deep neural networks.
The encoders-and-ensembles architecture will outperform prior state-of-the-art methods in continual learning tasks, especially in settings with unknown task boundaries and online class learning.
An expansion-based approach for task-free continual learning will effectively eliminate catastrophic forgetting by allocating new resources to learn new data.
Super-convergence allows neural networks to be trained an order of magnitude faster than with standard training methods.

Few-shot class-incremental learning (FSCIL) algorithms can effectively learn new concepts from limited data while mitigating catastrophic forgetting through a decoupled learning strategy and a Continually Evolved Classifier.
A novel continual learning approach can handle both learning and storing new information, as well as revisiting and adapting to changes in previously seen concepts.
Embedding networks suffer significantly less from catastrophic forgetting compared to classification networks.
Large numbers of hidden nodes and dense feature extraction are critical to achieving high performance in single-layer networks for unsupervised feature learning.
A strategy built on pseudo-labeling, consistency regularization, Out-of-Distribution detection, and knowledge distillation reduces forgetting in the semi-supervised continual learning setting.
Using experience replay buffers for all past events can substantially reduce catastrophic forgetting in continual learning tasks.
Cumulative training strategy with Net2Net transformation achieves training computational efficiency without incurring large accuracy loss compared to training from scratch.
A very small episodic memory in continual learning setup significantly outperforms specifically designed continual learning approaches.
Forgetting can in fact be favorable to learning in artificial neural networks.
Prototype augmentation and self-supervised learning will significantly improve performance in incremental learning compared to non-exemplar based methods.

Memory-based Parameter Adaptation will allow neural networks to adapt more quickly and effectively to training distribution shifts by using higher learning rates and alleviating issues such as catastrophic forgetting.

A comprehensive experimental comparison of 10 state-of-the-art continual learning methods will determine which method performs best on balanced Tiny Imagenet and a large-scale unbalanced iNaturalist datasets.

ARPER (Adaptively Regularized Prioritized Exemplar Replay) significantly outperforms other methods by effectively mitigating the detrimental catastrophic forgetting issue in continual learning for natural language generation.
Simple baselines can achieve similar performance to current mainstream methods in continual learning scenarios.
Dark Experience Replay will outperform consolidated approaches in General Continual Learning settings.

Universally slimmable networks (US-Nets) will improve performance on ImageNet classification, image super-resolution, and deep reinforcement learning tasks compared to individually trained networks and 4-switch slimmable network baselines.
Controlled sampling of memories for replay will produce consistent gains in performance and greatly reduced forgetting in online continual learning.

The transformer architecture based on a dedicated encoder/decoder framework will efficiently reduce catastrophic forgetting in continual learning while scaling to a large number of tasks with negligible memory and time overheads.
The consistent representation learning method can better learn consistent representations to alleviate forgetting effectively.
Conditional channel gated networks will improve performance in continual learning tasks by preventing catastrophic forgetting.
The Consensus Network (ConNet) framework achieves significant improvements over existing methods in multi-source learning settings.
Self-attention-based architectures will improve federated learning over heterogeneous data by reducing catastrophic forgetting and accelerating convergence.
Continuing pre-training could lead to the pre-trained model’s catastrophic forgetting, and a learning method with less forgetting can alleviate this issue.
The recall and learn mechanism will reduce catastrophic forgetting in fine-tuning deep pretrained language models, leading to improved performance on downstream tasks.
Trainable subnetworks exist in pre-trained vision-and-language models that can maintain high accuracy with significant sparsity.
OML minimizes catastrophic interference by learning representations that accelerate future learning and are robust to forgetting under online updates in continual learning.
Deep Generative Replay will alleviate catastrophic forgetting in continual learning by enabling the sampling of training data for previous tasks using a cooperative dual model architecture.
Neural Example Extrapolation (Ex2) will significantly improve performance on few-shot learning benchmarks compared to state-of-the-art methods.

The incremental learning algorithm for the Multilayer Perceptron will incorporate new knowledge without exhaustive re-training, achieving efficiency comparable to a network that is exhaustively trained.

The architectural method based on residual adapters and a simple replay-based strategy perform better compared to other continuous learning techniques and only slightly worse than the multitask learning upper bound while being 20X faster in learning new domains.
The CTR model will effectively address catastrophic forgetting and encourage knowledge transfer in continual learning.

given_idea: Q-Diversity can consistently improve worst-case accuracy under different distributional shifts, outperforming state-of-the-art alternatives.
original: NA

top_k.sentence: Promoting ensemble diversity via an adaptive diversity promoting regularizer will improve adversarial robustness for ensemble models while maintaining state-of-the-art accuracy on normal examples.
Learning strategies for deep ensembles need to tackle the trade-off between ensemble diversity and individual accuracies.
Composition Sampling generates diverse outputs for conditional generation of higher quality compared to previous stochastic decoding strategies.
Increasing both the training set and model sizes significantly improve the distributional shift robustness.
The proposed estimator exhibits improved bias-variance trade-offs on standard benchmark tasks.
The novel method of over-sampling candidates and using clustering to remove similar sequences will achieve high diversity without sacrificing quality in outputs from conditional language models.
Modifications in system design choices and hyperparameter optimizations can improve traditional distributional models to yield performance gains similar to those of word embeddings.
The proposed distribution matching approach consistently outperforms previous methods by a large margin.
Enforcing diversity in the sampled batches using determinantal point processes can improve the predictive performance of dependency parsers compared to diversity-agnostic counterparts.
Diversity Training significantly improves the adversarial robustness of ensembles and can be combined with existing methods to create a stronger defense.
Strongly-typed architectures achieve lower training and comparable generalization error to classical architectures.
A learned conditional masking mechanism enables the model to strongly generalize far outside of its training range with near-perfect accuracy on a variety of algorithms.
Diversity-promoting question generation provides better question answering training than likelihood maximization approaches.
BatchEnsemble achieves lower computational and memory costs compared to typical ensembles while yielding competitive accuracy and uncertainties.

Pre-Training improves generalization by achieving flatter loss landscapes than Random-Initialization, and improves negative diversity by assigning smoother lexical probability distributions than Random-Initialization.
Quadapter effectively prevents overfitting and improves quantization performance for GPT-2.
Completely derandomized self-adaptation scheme adapts arbitrary normal mutation distributions and can be improved by cumulation.

A continuum of lower bounds that encompasses previous bounds and flexibly trades off bias and variance will improve the estimation and representation learning of Mutual Information in high-dimensional problems.
Learning fair representations admits an information-theoretic lower bound on the joint error across different population groups.
Learning to generate compilable code as constraint satisfaction improves compilability rates without sacrificing diversity and complexity of the generated samples.
Using multiple diverse discriminators in adversarial learning will substantially improve bias reduction and training stability compared to standard adversarial removal methods.

The proposed diversity loss maximizes the ratio of pairwise distance between the generated texts and their corresponding latent codes, forcing the generator to focus more on the latent codes and produce diverse samples.
The expressiveness of Softmax-based models is limited by a Softmax bottleneck, and the proposed method improves the state-of-the-art perplexities on Penn Treebank and WikiText-2.
Symmetric quantization can substantially improve accuracy for networks with extremely low-precision weights and activations.
Counterfactually augmented data may limit the model's effectiveness on out-of-distribution generalization due to lack of perturbation diversity.
Framing the distribution mismatch problem as domain adaptation will yield clear improvement over strong baselines in both supervised and unsupervised adaptation scenarios.
The shortcut mitigation framework LGTR can improve the generalization accuracy on out-of-distribution data while preserving the accuracy on in-distribution data.
Switch-based Active Deep Dyna-Q leads to significant improvement over Deep Dyna-Q and Q-learning baselines in both simulation and human evaluations.
Prototype-driven learning for sequence models provides substantial error rate reductions over competitive baselines and outperforms previous work.
Models trained with the resulting parametric adversaries are consistently more robust to subpopulation shifts when compared to other Distributionally Robust Optimization approaches.
Differentiable Markov Channel Pruning (DMCP) will achieve consistent improvement compared to state-of-the-art pruning methods in various FLOPs settings.

Increased regularization allows distributionally robust optimization models to achieve substantially higher worst-group accuracies, with 10% to 40% improvements over standard models on a natural language inference task and two image tasks.
Invariant Risk Minimization and Spectral Decoupling outperform sampling-based approaches to class imbalance and concept drift, leading to much better performance on minority classes.
The Frequency-Aware Cross-Entropy loss function will substantially improve the diversity of existing state-of-the-art Seq2Seq response generation methods.
The discrete key-value bottleneck minimizes the effect of learning under distribution shifts and reduces the complexity of the hypothesis class.
Out-of-Distribution generalization algorithms show a large room for improvement on time series tasks.
Fine-tuning with a diverse set of hard positives improves the robustness of networks compared to training with prior methods of generating adversarial images.
The quantization based approach will significantly outperform existing state-of-the-art methods for fast approximate Maximum Inner Product Search.
On-the-fly Native Ensemble (ONE) improves the generalisation performance of a variety of deep neural networks more significantly than alternative methods.
Orthant-Wise Limited-memory Quasi-Newton (OWL-QN) can efficiently optimize the L1-regularized log-likelihood of log-linear models with millions of parameters.
The adaptation of the Double Q-learning algorithm to the Deep Q-Network algorithm reduces overestimations and leads to better performance on several games.
Averaging the weights of multiple models fine-tuned with different hyperparameter configurations improves accuracy and robustness without increasing inference or memory costs.
Infinite mixture prototypes will improve accuracy and robustness in few-shot learning compared to existing prototypical methods.
The inclusion of both training and inference diversity terms improves performance, out-performing current state-of-the-art systems in terms of ROUGE scores.
CORE counterfactuals are more effective at improving generalization to out-of-distribution data compared to other data augmentation approaches.
Out-of-distribution generalization largely depends on the expansion function.
Contrastive Active Learning (CAL) achieves a better trade-off between uncertainty and diversity compared to other strategies.

COD3S improves output diversity by conditioning generation on locality-sensitive hash-based semantic sentence codes whose Hamming distances correlate with human judgments of semantic textual similarity.
Neural sequence models trained with maximum likelihood estimation demonstrate challenges in achieving robustness, compositionality, and out-of-distribution generalization.
PathFid leads to strong performance gains on two multi-hop QA datasets: HotpotQA and IIRC.

given_idea: Open-Modality Speech Recognition (OpenSR) enables modality transfer from one to any in three different settings (zero-, few- and full-shot) and achieves highly competitive zero-shot performance compared to the existing few-shot and full-shot lip-reading methods.
original: NA

top_k.sentence: The new approach for zero-shot cross-modal transfer between speech and text significantly improves the state-of-the-art for zero-shot speech translation.
openSMILE 2.0 unites feature extraction paradigms from speech, music, and general sound events with basic video features for multi-modal processing.
Leveraging visual modality-specific representations will enhance the performance of audio-visual speech recognition in noisy conditions compared to traditional modality-invariant approaches.
Cross-modal distillation from an Automatic Speech Recognition model enables training of lip reading systems without requiring human annotated ground truth data.
A cross-modal student-teacher learning framework will improve speech recognition performance under low signal-to-noise-ratio and acoustic mismatch conditions.
Jointly modeling automatic speech recognition tokens and visual features results in higher performance compared to training individually on either modality.

Pre-training an audio-text multimodal model with extremely low-resource parallel data and extra non-parallel unimodal data will achieve comparable performance on multiple downstream speech understanding tasks compared with the model pre-trained on fully parallel data.
Discrete Cross-Modal Alignment method will significantly improve zero-shot speech translation performance compared to existing methods.
Voice conversion can be used cross-lingually to improve low-resource speech recognition.

Learning visual speech Representations from Audio via self-supervision (LiRA) will significantly outperform other self-supervised methods on the Lip Reading in the Wild (LRW) dataset and achieve state-of-the-art performance on Lip Reading Sentences 2 (LRS2) using only a fraction of the total labelled data.

Bimodal deep fusion state machine network will improve the robustness of audio-visual speech recognition in noisy environments through optimal integration of acoustic and visual information, multi-condition training, and dropout regularization.
Multimodal one-shot learning will enable a robot to match new continuous speech input to the correct visual instance after seeing one paired audiovisual example per class.
The proposed end-to-end, multitask learning audiovisual automatic speech recognition system will achieve the best performance across all conditions for both speech tasks.
The audio-visual speech recognition system will significantly reduce error rates in noisy environments compared to traditional acoustic-only methods.

Censer, a semi-supervised speech recognition algorithm based on self-supervised pre-training, will maximize the utilization of unlabeled data and achieve better performance compared to existing approaches.

Mapping the sequence of mouth movement images directly to mel-spectrogram will reconstruct speech relevant information and outperform state-of-the-art models on the GRID benchmark lip reading dataset.
Acoustic-to-Word recognition provides a straightforward solution to end-to-end speech recognition without needing external decoding, language model re-scoring or lexicon.
Novel end-to-end multimodal Automatic Speech Recognition systems will perform better than adaptive training approaches, although improvements are slightly less.
End-to-end training, the use of a conformer, and the use of a transformer-based language model significantly improve the performance of the model in audio-visual speech recognition.
Cross-modal Speech and Language Model (SpeechLM) will significantly improve speech pre-training by aligning speech and text modalities using unpaired textual data.
The speaker augmentation approach can significantly reduce word error rate (WER) in low resource speech recognition tasks compared to systems without data augmentation.
The direct acoustic-to-word, end-to-end speech recognition model utilizing conversational context outperforms a standard end-to-end speech recognition system.
Leveraging unimodal self-supervised learning will promote the multimodal audio-visual speech recognition.
Universal ASR will provide an efficient mechanism to integrate streaming and non-streaming models that can recognize speech quickly and accurately.
A dialog-context aware speech recognition model will improve recognition accuracy compared to a sentence-level end-to-end speech recognition system.
Visual Context-driven Audio Feature Enhancement module (V-CAFE) can improve the noise-robustness of Audio-Visual Speech Recognition (AVSR) systems.
Modality-Specific Learning Rate (MSLR) method will outperform global learning rates on multiple tasks and settings in late-fusion multimodal models.
The multimodal information bottleneck approach will improve performance on image-to-speech synthesis and traditional cross-modal generation tasks using unpaired data.
Multimodal deep learning will improve feature learning across modalities, leading to better performance in tasks such as audio-visual speech classification.

Fusing a pre-trained acoustic encoder (wav2vec2.0) and a pre-trained linguistic encoder (BERT) into an end-to-end automatic speech recognition model will improve recognition performance on low-resource tasks.

Adopting a meta-learning algorithm to train a modality agnostic multi-task model will significantly improve the performance of end-to-end Speech Translation tasks compared to previous transfer learning approaches.
A robust spoken language understanding system leveraging both acoustic and domain knowledge will outperform existing systems in robustness to automatic speech recognition errors and data sparsity.
Learning modality-invariant representations for speech and images will effectively filter out modality information while preserving semantic information.
Adopting a meta-learning algorithm to train a modality agnostic multi-task model will significantly improve performance on speech translation tasks compared to previous transfer learning approaches.
The asymmetric acoustic model with phone set extension and the integrated language model will reduce the word error rate in mixed language speech recognition compared to conventional models.
Hidden Markov models can be effectively used to solve distinct problems in speech recognition.
An attention-based pooling mechanism and sub-word units for lip reading will significantly improve the performance of lip reading models in recognizing speech from silent videos.
Multi-head Visual-audio Memory (MVM) will effectively distinguish homophenes and improve lip reading performance by utilizing audio representations to complement visual information.
Utterance-level permutation invariant training (uPIT) will outperform techniques based on nonnegative matrix factorization and computational auditory scene analysis for multitalker speech separation.
The proposed system can predict intents from flexible types of inputs: speech, automatic speech recognition transcripts, or both, achieving better results when combining modalities.
A novel collaborative framework for lip reading will outperform state-of-the-art methods by effectively distilling knowledge from a master network that integrates audio and video modalities.

Combining components from state-of-the-art open-domain dialogue agents with those from state-of-the-art vision models will result in a model that outperforms existing models in multi-modal dialogue while maintaining performance in text-based conversation.

The self-supervised audio-visual speech recognition framework built upon Audio-Visual HuBERT outperforms prior state-of-the-art by ~50% using less than 10% of labeled data in the presence of babble noise.
Wav-BERT significantly outperforms existing approaches and achieves state-of-the-art performance on low-resource speech recognition.
DeCoAR 2.0 will improve speech representation learning and outperform models trained on full datasets when using limited labeled data.
SpecAugment will achieve state-of-the-art performance on automatic speech recognition tasks compared to prior methods.
The proposed approach can learn transferable, robust, and problem-agnostic features that carry on relevant information from the speech signal.

The Skeleton Aware Multi-modal Framework with a Global Ensemble Model (GEM) for isolated Sign Language Recognition (SAM-SLR-v2) will achieve state-of-the-art performance with significant margins compared to existing methods.
The zero-shot method for audio-visual keyword spotting improves performance in identifying spoken words in videos, with or without audio.
The unsupervised ASR-error adaptation method can yield significant improvement over strong baselines and achieve performance very close to the oracle system.

given_idea: Instruction tuning can achieve superior results in zero-shot and few-shot scenarios and obtain the best performance on the fully-supervised tasks in StoryWars.
original: NA

top_k.sentence: Instruction tuning substantially improves zero-shot performance on unseen tasks for language models.
MultiInstruct will improve zero-shot performance on multimodal tasks through instruction tuning.
Instruction tuning is the key to the large language model’s zero-shot summarization capability.
The proposed framework learns to reliably accomplish program instructions and achieves zero-shot generalization to more complex instructions.

Using GPT-4 to generate instruction-following data for large language model finetuning leads to superior zero-shot performance on new tasks compared to instruction-following data generated by previous state-of-the-art models.
Meta-tuning directly optimizes the zero-shot learning objective by fine-tuning pre-trained language models on a collection of datasets.
Pre-trained prompt tuning (PPT) can reach or even outperform full-model fine-tuning under both full-data and few-shot settings.

Instruction finetuning with scaling the number of tasks, scaling the model size, and finetuning on chain-of-thought data dramatically improves performance on a variety of model classes, prompting setups, and evaluation benchmarks.
Continual training of an instruction-following agent from user feedback will improve instruction execution accuracy over time.
Mapping raw visual observations and text input to actions for instruction execution will significantly improve performance over supervised learning and common reinforcement learning variants.
Instruction-tuning decisions significantly affect downstream task performance when scaling model and benchmark sizes.
Fine-tuning the zero-shot classifier on its most confident predictions leads to significant performance gains across a wide range of text classification tasks.
Training instruction-following agents with deep reinforcement learning will achieve robust performance with natural human instructions through zero-shot transfer from synthetic template commands.
A learning approach focused on recovery from cascading errors between instructions will improve the mapping of user instructions to system actions in collaborative scenarios.
Prompt-based learners can successfully be applied in true few-shot settings.
Language adaptation to be effective at improving zero-shot performance in new languages.
The proposed method outperforms previous methods by a large margin in interactive instruction-following tasks.
Few-shot instruction-based method for prompting pre-trained language models will detect different types of fine-grained biases with similar and sometimes superior accuracy to fine-tuned models.
RetroPrompt can obtain better performance in both few-shot and zero-shot settings and can yield better generalization abilities with new datasets.
InstructionNER will outperform other baselines on few-shot named entity recognition tasks.
VIMA will outperform alternative designs in zero-shot generalization settings by achieving a task success rate up to 2.9 times higher given the same training data.
PanGu-α will demonstrate superior capabilities in performing various tasks under few-shot or zero-shot settings compared to existing large-scale pretrained language models.

The instruction-tuned model outperforms single and multi-task baselines on an average by ~23% and ~6% across various evaluation settings, and more importantly, instruction-tuned model outperforms baselines by ~5% on cross-tasks.
Snapshot learning leads to consistent performance improvements independent of which architecture is used.
A comprehensive survey is given on the methodology, implementation, and fair evaluations for practical and applied computing facets on the recent progress of zero-shot learning.
Automated curriculum learning minimizes worst-case-aware losses across tasks, leading to better performance in zero-shot and few-shot transfer settings.
Decomposing instruction execution into goal prediction and action generation will enhance the model's ability to map raw visual observations to goals and generate required actions.
Finetuning language models in the few-shot setting can considerably reduce the need for prompt engineering and achieve competitive accuracy with null prompts.
Modeling zero-shot performance prediction as a multi-task learning problem will lead to more accurate predictors for tasks with limited test data.
Models benefit from instructions when evaluated in terms of generalization to unseen tasks.
Meta-learned Prompt Tuning (MetaPT) achieves better and more stable performance than the state-of-the-art method.
The proposed zero-shot learning model can achieve state-of-the-art results on both zero-shot learning and few-shot learning problems.
Scaling to 1,000 tasks can substantially improve training efficiency and zero-shot generalization performance.
Unified Prompt Tuning (UPT) achieves a better trade-off than the unimodal counterparts on few-shot learning benchmarks and domain generalization benchmarks.
A mining-based approach for zero-shot learning will outperform prompting language models on a wide range of tasks.
Tk-Instruct outperforms existing instruction-following models such as InstructGPT by over 9% on the Super-NaturalInstructions benchmark despite being an order of magnitude smaller.
Geometry-guided self-supervised learning method (Go-tuning) can enable T5-small (80M) competitive zero-shot results compared with large language models, such as T5-XL (3B).
Large language models can perform table-related tasks with few-shot in-context learning and achieve strong performance with only a 1-shot demonstration.
Reframed instructions lead to significant improvements in the performance of Language Models on various NLP tasks compared to original instructions.
Child-Tuning will consistently outperform vanilla fine-tuning and prior fine-tuning techniques in terms of performance on downstream tasks.

Properly optimized prompt tuning can be universally effective across a wide range of model scales and natural language understanding tasks, matching the performance of fine-tuning while having only 0.1%-3% tuned parameters.
The new training algorithm will effectively parse instructions from an unknown target domain, demonstrating substantial performance gains over a non-adapted parser.
Tip-Adapter performs comparably to training-required approaches for few-shot classification while inheriting the training-free advantage of zero-shot CLIP.
Ontology-enhanced prompt-tuning (OntoPrompt) will obtain better few-shot performance than baselines.
Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) offers better accuracy and dramatically lower computational costs compared to few-shot in-context learning (ICL).
The conditioning approach improves Workflow Discovery performance and enhances zero-shot and few-shot performance when transferring learned models to unseen domains.
ChatGPT outperforms large language models with zero-shot learning on most tasks and even outperforms fine-tuned models on some tasks.

The speaker-driven data augmentation, pragmatic reasoning, and panoramic action space components will dramatically improve the performance of a baseline instruction follower, more than doubling the success rate over the best existing approach on a standard benchmark.

Using both types of pretrained language models for fully zero-shot learning of natural language understanding tasks will significantly outperform zero-shot prompting methods and achieve comparable results to strong few-shot approaches.
The few-shot learning schemes significantly improve or outperform the commonly used baseline, a pre-trained language model-based linear classifier fine-tuned on domain labels.

given_idea: The classifier decomposition framework will consistently outperform state-of-the-art continual relation extraction models.
original: NA

top_k.sentence: Employing relation prototypes to extract useful information of each relation enhances continual relation extraction performance.
The discriminative reasoning framework will outperform the previous state-of-the-art performance on the large-scale document-level relation extraction dataset.
The proposed document-level relation extraction model with iterative inference will outperform existing methods in terms of accuracy and efficiency.
A framework that unifies the learning of Relation Extraction and Knowledge Base Embeddings models leads to significant improvements over the state-of-the-art in Relation Extraction.
A new paradigm of Relation Extraction that considers the predictions of all relations in the same context will outperform state-of-the-art approaches.
The Continual Relation Extraction framework with Contrastive Learning (CRECL) will alleviate the catastrophic forgetting problem and improve performance in continual relation extraction tasks.
Curriculum-meta learning method will effectively tackle catastrophic forgetting and order-sensitivity challenges in continual relation extraction.
Generating knowledge-infused relation prototypes will leverage the relational knowledge from pre-trained language models to improve continual relation extraction.
The proposed approach based on lifelong learning and optimization-based meta-learning will markedly outperform current state-of-the-art approaches in lifelong relation extraction.
The indicator-aware approach for relation extraction will significantly outperform state-of-the-art methods by leveraging syntactic indicators and sentential contexts.
The proposed method can significantly outperform previous state-of-the-art methods in continual few-shot relation learning task settings.
A joint approach for entity-level relation extraction using multi-instance learning will achieve state-of-the-art results and be more efficient compared to task-specific learning.
Combining distant and partial supervision for relation extraction will yield a substantial increase in end-to-end F1 score on the 2013 KBP Slot Filling evaluation.
Relation Extraction with Distant Supervision and Transformers (REDSandT) captures a broader set of relations with higher confidence compared to existing approaches.
Graph Aggregation-and-Inference Network (GAIN) achieves a significant performance improvement (2.85 on F1) over the previous state-of-the-art in document-level relation extraction.

Leveraging full dependency forests for relation extraction will provide significant improvements over existing methods, demonstrating state-of-the-art or competitive performances on biomedical and newswire benchmarks.
The Feature-rich Compositional Embedding Model (FCM) will outperform both previous compositional models and traditional feature-rich models on relation extraction tasks.
Exploiting syntactico-semantic structures for relation extraction will provide significant improvement in relation extraction performance.
ARNOR framework achieves significant improvements in relation classification performance and noise reduction effect compared to state-of-the-art methods.
Incorporating relation paths in neural relation extraction will achieve significant and consistent improvements on relation extraction compared to existing methods.
TPLinker performs significantly better on overlapping and multiple relation extraction, and achieves state-of-the-art performance on two public datasets.
A globally optimized neural model for end-to-end relation extraction will achieve better performance compared to local classification methods.
A single, joint graphical model will consistently improve results on entity tagging, relation extraction, and coreference compared to isolated models.
The semi-supervised relation extraction system with large-scale word clustering outperformed a state-of-the-art supervised baseline system.
The multi-lingual neural relation extraction framework will achieve significant improvements on relation extraction compared to existing methods.
The simple baseline approach (PARE) outperforms existing state-of-the-art distantly supervised relation extraction models in both monolingual and multilingual datasets.
A history-based structured learning approach jointly extracts entities and relations in a sentence and significantly outperforms a pipeline approach by incorporating global features.
Injecting logical background knowledge into embeddings for relation extraction will enable accurate extractors to be learned with little or no distant supervision alignments.
The path-based representation learning model will achieve significant and consistent improvements on knowledge base completion and relation extraction from text compared to existing methods.
The contrastive instance learning framework will significantly improve the performance of distantly supervised relation extraction models compared to the multi-instance learning framework.
The joint embedding model will achieve significant improvement on relation extraction compared to cutting-edge methods.
The unified joint extraction model will significantly improve the extraction of overlapping relations and long-range relations compared to existing methods.
The new approach to relation extraction can reliably extract relations from web documents using minimal supervision.
The proposed SSAN model significantly outperforms competitive baselines in document-level relation extraction tasks.
The proposed encoder-classifier-reconstructor model for document-level relation extraction will significantly improve the accuracy of relation extraction compared to existing graph-based models.
The recurrent interaction network will improve the performance of joint entity extraction and relation classification compared to existing multi-task learning methods.
Fine-grained entity recognition will increase the F1 score of relation extraction systems by 93%.
The novel approach for multi-instance learning with overlapping relations will yield significant gains in accuracy for information extraction tasks.
The inter-sentence relation extraction model will effectively utilize local and non-local dependencies to improve performance on relation extraction tasks.
The proposed model achieves superior performance on two public datasets for document-level relation extraction.
The few-shot learning framework for relation classification will outperform state-of-the-art results given small training data.
A convolutional neural network for relation extraction will significantly outperform traditional relation extraction systems that rely on linguistic analysis features.
The proposed relation extractor architecture exploits the implicit knowledge of a language model through a question-answering approach.
An unsupervised method for relation discovery from large corpora will detect relations among named entities with high recall and precision.
Learning relation prototypes from unlabeled texts will facilitate long-tail relation extraction by transferring knowledge from relation types with sufficient training data.
Matrix completion on factorized matrix of minimized rank will significantly improve relation extraction performance in the presence of sparse and noisy features.
Adversarial class augmentation mechanism will improve the robustness of representations in continual relation extraction models, thereby mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
A neural, end-to-end model for jointly extracting entities and their relations will match or exceed state-of-the-art performance without relying on external natural language processing tools.
The span-level model will achieve state-of-the-art performance in relation extraction tasks compared to token-level models.

Incorporating the hierarchical information of relations for distantly supervised relation extraction will significantly improve the identification of valid instances, especially for long-tail relations.

given_idea: Drift in the classifier leads to forgetting in class-incremental learning.
original: NA

top_k.sentence: Few-shot class-incremental learning (FSCIL) algorithms can effectively learn new concepts from limited data while mitigating catastrophic forgetting through a decoupled learning strategy and a Continually Evolved Classifier.
Annotation noise can severely degrade the generalization ability of a linear classifier.
Sequential learning in multilayer connectionist models leads to rapid forgetting of well-learned information and nonmonotonic discrimination between studied and new items.
The mechanisms that mitigate catastrophic forgetting in neural networks vary based on the incremental training paradigm and type of data being used.
Connectionist networks can forget previously learned information suddenly and completely when learning new information.
The dynamically expandable representation for class incremental learning will achieve better stability-plasticity trade-off and outperform existing methods in incremental concept modeling.

A causal framework can explain the catastrophic forgetting in Class-Incremental Learning and the proposed causal effect distillation can improve various state-of-the-art Class-Incremental Learning methods by a large margin (0.72%–9.06%).
Forgetting can in fact be favorable to learning in artificial neural networks.
Repetition can significantly ameliorate the effects of catastrophic forgetting in incremental object learning.
Orthogonal Gradient Descent method will effectively restrict the direction of gradient updates to avoid catastrophic forgetting in neural networks during continual learning.
The Incremental Delta-Bar-Delta algorithm performs better than ordinary LMS in drifting or non-stationary learning tasks and finds the optimal learning rates.
A strategy built on pseudo-labeling, consistency regularization, Out-of-Distribution detection, and knowledge distillation reduces forgetting in the semi-supervised continual learning setting.
Different types of active learning algorithms produce an inconsistent gain over random sampling baseline under identical experimental settings.
The proposed incremental prototype learning scheme will enhance the performance of few-shot class-incremental learning by improving representation optimization and prototype reorganization.
The less-forgetting learning method is effective in alleviating the catastrophic forgetting problem in deep neural networks.
The consistent representation learning method can better learn consistent representations to alleviate forgetting effectively.
Sparse neural Network for Continual Learning (SNCL) achieves state-of-the-art result for mitigating forgetting.
Injecting additive noise at each time step during training will improve convergence and generalization performance in recurrent neural networks.
An active learning algorithm approximately maximizes recall of the classifier while respecting a precision constraint with provably sub-linear label complexity.
A very small episodic memory in continual learning setup significantly outperforms specifically designed continual learning approaches.
Iteratively learning embeddings and rules benefits each other during learning and prediction.
The generalization bound establishes fast convergence of the expected risk of a distillation-trained linear classifier.
The relative generalization gap of in-context learning decreases as models are scaled up.
Prototype augmentation and self-supervised learning will significantly improve performance in incremental learning compared to non-exemplar based methods.

Certain examples are forgotten with high frequency, and some not at all; a data set’s (un)forgettable examples generalize across neural architectures; and based on forgetting dynamics, a significant fraction of examples can be omitted from the training data set while still maintaining state-of-the-art generalization performance.
The gradual forgetting of Time-Varying Gaussian Process Upper Confidence Bound performs favorably compared to the sharp resetting of Reset Gaussian Process Upper Confidence Bound.
Temporal concept drift impacts explanation faithfulness of model predictions in natural language processing.

A novel incremental distillation strategy for Data-Free Class-Incremental Learning will result in up to a 25.1% increase in final task accuracy compared to state-of-the-art Data-Free Class-Incremental Learning methods for common class-incremental benchmarks.
Weight decay can improve generalization in a feed-forward neural network by suppressing irrelevant components of the weight vector and reducing the effects of static noise on targets.
Active learning improves the rate of learning in scenarios with sparse and strongly biased data compared to conventional passive learning.
Embedding networks suffer significantly less from catastrophic forgetting compared to classification networks.

Augmenting an array of independent memory slots with a learnable random graph that captures pairwise similarities between samples will guard against catastrophic forgetting in continual learning models.
OML minimizes catastrophic interference by learning representations that accelerate future learning and are robust to forgetting under online updates in continual learning.
Warm-starting neural network training yields poorer generalization performance than models with fresh random initializations, despite similar final training losses.
Learning long-term dependencies in recurrent networks using gradient descent is a very difficult task.
Fine-tuning affects classification performance by increasing the distances between examples associated with different labels.
Existing group invariant learning methods can violate criteria for generalizing to spurious correlation shifts.
Controlled sampling of memories for replay will produce consistent gains in performance and greatly reduced forgetting in online continual learning.
K-Adapter improves the performance of pre-trained models by allowing continual knowledge infusion without catastrophic forgetting.
Unlabeled data improves training convergence and generalization ability in self-training algorithms.
An expansion-based approach for task-free continual learning will effectively eliminate catastrophic forgetting by allocating new resources to learn new data.
Invariant Risk Minimization and Spectral Decoupling outperform sampling-based approaches to class imbalance and concept drift, leading to much better performance on minority classes.
Memory-augmented neural networks can rapidly assimilate new data and make accurate predictions after only a few samples.
Regression-based classifiers perform better than binary classifiers because they preserve more information about annotator judgements.
Gradient based learning algorithms face an increasingly difficult problem as the duration of the dependencies to be captured increases.
Early-learning regularization prevents memorization of noisy labels in deep learning classification tasks.
Memorization of labels is necessary for achieving close-to-optimal generalization error for natural data distributions.
Learning longer term patterns in real data is possible using gradient descent with a structural modification of the simple recurrent neural network architecture.
Applying dropout to Long Short-Term Memory units in Recurrent Neural Networks substantially reduces overfitting on various tasks.
A new learning framework for robust and efficient fine-tuning for pre-trained models will attain better generalization performance.

given_idea: Incorporating the relationship between inputs and possible outputs into learning will achieve a deeper semantic understanding of programs.
original: NA

top_k.sentence: A novel program semantics learning paradigm will improve the understanding of programs by learning from representations aligned with operational semantics and environment transitions.
Using graphs to represent both the syntactic and semantic structure of code will improve reasoning over program structures in learning tasks.
In order for neural architectures to robustly learn program semantics, it is necessary to incorporate a concept like recursion.
Understanding semantic capacity of terms will help many downstream tasks in natural language processing.
A method that integrates compositional semantics into learning performs better than all other alternatives.
The representation introduced allows to infer interesting semantic relationships between queries.
Incorporating semantic information in the training process will enhance the performance of language models on natural language understanding tasks.
Separating syntactic from semantic learning may be a useful heuristic for capturing compositional structure.
The new collection of semantic relation pairs will be of considerable use in computational areas in which semantic relations play a role.
Semantic Vector Networks will capture aspects of word meaning that are complementary to what is captured by traditional word embeddings.
Applying an embedding-based method will effectively learn prerequisite relations for course concepts in Natural Language Processing.
RC-NET will produce word representations of higher quality by leveraging both relational and categorical knowledge.

Combining ConceptNet with word embeddings acquired from distributional semantics will improve natural language applications by providing understanding that is not achievable from distributional semantics alone.
Transfer learning techniques in natural language processing will effectively leverage pre-existing knowledge for improved learning outcomes.
Integrating statistical and relational learning techniques for natural language interfaces will be more robust than a purely logic-based approach.
The proposed semantics can be learned and dynamically updated through experience.
Long Short-Term Memory units can learn to map character-level representations of computer programs to their correct outputs.
The neural semantic parsing system will learn new high-level abstractions through decomposition more efficiently and reliably than existing methods.
Incorporating prior knowledge from semantic resources will improve lexical semantic embeddings compared to embeddings learned solely on raw text.
Pretraining with an artificial language with a nesting dependency structure provides some knowledge transferable to natural language.
A flexible combination of pattern recognition and explicit reasoning can be used to solve complex programming problems.
Augmenting the input of the classifier with appropriate semantic category information results in significant improvements to classification accuracy.
A simple discriminative learning framework will effectively define statistical models that learn compositional semantic theories from corpora and databases.
Indirect supervision of program learning via answer rationales is a promising strategy for inducing arithmetic programs.

Using dictionary definitions as a means of bridging the gap between lexical and phrasal semantics will enable neural language embedding models to effectively learn representations of phrases and sentences.
The associative knowledge network model will provide a more interpretable semantic representation of noun context compared to existing deep neural network methods.
A methodology for distilling relational knowledge from a pre-trained language model will effectively capture relational knowledge beyond what is captured by standard word embeddings.
The proposed domain alignment learns a semantically meaningful representation which outperforms previous work.

Learning a semantic parser from question–answer pairs, where the logical form is modeled as a latent variable, will achieve comparable accuracies to state-of-the-art systems that require annotated logical forms.
Textual relation embedding can facilitate downstream tasks requiring relational understanding of the text.
Incorporating syntax allows a concise model to learn representations that are both effective and general.
The proposed approach of representing the input as word pairs achieves state-of-the-art results on four-way classification of both implicit and explicit relations.
Introducing a variety of syntactically and semantically motivated features will improve the accuracy of temporal relation classification tasks.
Injecting semantic knowledge, in particular, coreference information, into an existing model would improve performance on complex problems.
A robust model should transgress the initial notion of semantic similarity induced by word embeddings to learn a more human-like understanding of meaning.
Semantic content can be computed using the Kullback-Leibler divergence.
Using consistency between output programs for related inputs will reduce the impact of spurious programs in weakly supervised semantic parsing.
A unified framework will effectively enhance semantic role labeling performance by incorporating high-quality syntactic input.
Enhanced and enhanced++ English Universal Dependencies representations will provide improved relations between content words for natural language understanding tasks.
Mapping to pre-learned representations substantially improves performance over systems whose representations are learned from limited instructional data alone.
Recurrent neural networks are able to simultaneously learn about structural and semantic relations between words.

The proposed representation learning-based method for learning latent representations of course concepts achieves significant improvements in inferring prerequisite relations compared to existing methods.
Explicit representation of spatial concepts and relationships can improve the capabilities of machine learning models in reasoning and deep language understanding.
Inductive logic programming (ILP) induces a hypothesis that generalises given training examples and background knowledge.
The joint parsing of syntactic and semantic dependencies will unify and extend previous shared tasks in natural language processing.
The synthesized data generated from our model can substantially help a semantic parser achieve better compositional and domain generalization.

The semantic parsing framework with the dual learning algorithm will enable a semantic parser to make full use of data through a dual-learning game, achieving new state-of-the-art performance on the ATIS dataset.
Leveraging natural language relations between answer choices will improve the performance of machine comprehension.
Incorporating contextual information into word representations prior to processing the task will significantly improve natural language understanding performance.
It is possible to extract knowledge of actions and objects from language and that joint inference over different types of knowledge improves performance.

given_idea: ACCENT is an efficient metric for event commonsense evaluation, which achieves higher correlations with human judgments than existing baselines.
original: NA

top_k.sentence: The automatic evaluation metric Q^2 will show higher correlation with human judgments compared to other metrics for evaluating factual consistency in knowledge-grounded dialogue.
UniEval will achieve a higher correlation with human judgments than existing metrics for multi-dimensional evaluation in Natural Language Generation.
The proposed evaluator achieves a strong correlation (> 0.6) with human judgement and generalizes robustly to diverse responses and corpora.

DEAM, a Dialogue coherence Evaluation metric that relies on Abstract Meaning Representation (AMR) to apply semantic-level Manipulations for incoherent data generation, achieves higher correlations with human judgments compared to baseline methods.
The automatic evaluation metric developed in this paper will provide a consistent estimator of fluency, outperforming individual parser metrics.

Measuring the performance of a dialogue system by computing the distribution-wise distance between its generated conversations and real-world conversations will correlate better with human judgments than existing metrics.
Interpretable metrics for evaluating topic coherence will serve as a surrogate for human judgment in assessing the quality of responses in dialogue systems.
The automatic evaluation model based on conversational context will outperform existing evaluation metrics in terms of high correlation with human annotation scores.

QuestEval substantially improves the correlation with human judgments over four evaluation dimensions (consistency, coherence, fluency, and relevance) compared to established metrics such as ROUGE or BERTScore.
Automated metrics have stronger correlation with human judgments in the task-oriented setting compared to what has been observed in the non task-oriented setting.
The PARENT metric correlates with human judgments better than existing text generation metrics.
The new evaluation measure will significantly outperform state-of-the-art approaches in sentence-level correlation.
The ACP-based models will outperform the baselines in the CommonsenseQA task.
BERTScore correlates better with human judgments and provides stronger model selection performance than existing metrics.
GRADE will significantly outperform other state-of-the-art metrics on measuring diverse dialogue models in terms of the Pearson and Spearman correlations with human judgements.
Item Response Theory will provide a more insightful evaluation of NLP systems compared to standard evaluation metrics.
The method for evaluating coherence between user utterances and those generated from open domain conversational systems significantly outperforms the baseline.
COLA (Contextualized Commonsense Causality Reasoner) can detect commonsense causality more accurately than baselines.
Incorporating cultural differences in commonsense reasoning datasets will improve the performance of machine reasoning in natural language processing tasks.
Determining granularity along semantic event slots improves precision and solves event coreference with scores comparable to those achieved in related work.
Three smoothing techniques correlate better with human judgments on the sentence-level than other smoothing techniques.
CIDER will improve the understanding and explanation of human conversations through contextual commonsense inference.
A new and improved word frequency measure for American English will provide more accurate estimates of word frequency effects in cognitive processing compared to existing norms.
Learning an automatic scoring metric based on human judgments will improve the evaluation of summaries in the summarization field.
New combinations of components outperform existing measures with respect to correlation to human ratings.
A speaker-aware extension on the best-performing model leads to gains in both the standard and conversational evaluation settings.
D-score will outperform the best baseline in terms of system-level Spearman correlation for dialogue evaluation.
The static attention based approach outperforms all the baselines on automatic and human evaluation.
Reference-less grammaticality metrics correlate very strongly with human judgments and are competitive with the leading reference-based evaluation metrics.
A knowledge-enhanced pretraining model for commonsense story generation will generate more reasonable stories than state-of-the-art baselines, particularly in terms of logic and global coherence.
Commonsense reasoning can improve performance on natural language processing tasks.
AutoJudge correlates well with human ratings and can be used to automatically evaluate dialogue systems.
Deep learning-based assessment models will predict human ratings of converted speech more accurately than existing objective evaluation metrics.
Language Model Augmented Relevance Score (MARS) achieves higher correlation with human reference judgements compared to existing metrics.
AIPNet will outperform accent-dependent and accent-independent models in terms of word error rate for multi-accent speech recognition.
The context-aware natural language generator yields significant improvements over the baseline in both automatic metrics and a human pairwise preference test.
Commonsense properties are features that humans can extract and characterize reliably by using their commonsense knowledge and native language skills.

Leveraging external commonsense knowledge about the intent and sentiment of events will significantly improve event embeddings for tasks such as event similarity, script event prediction, and stock market prediction.
Attention weights capture better word alignment than the leave-one-out measures under our setting.
The multi-faceted model of commonsense knowledge statements will improve the reasoning and ranking of inter-related statements compared to prior isolated approaches.

The multi-modal framework for evaluating English word representations based on cognitive lexical semantics will show strong correlations between cognitive datasets, across recording modalities, and their performance on extrinsic natural language processing tasks.
A novel metric to evaluate the factual consistency in text summarization via counterfactual estimation will improve the correlation with human judgments and the convenience of usage.
MoverScore shows a high correlation with human judgment of text quality across various text generation tasks.
Using a semantic concordance for sense identification will improve the accuracy of sense identification strategies compared to guessing and most-frequent heuristics.
NANO significantly improves dialect robustness while preserving the correlation between automated metrics and human ratings.
Acquiring commonsense knowledge through human computation dramatically improves the performance of established sentiment classification methods.
Flexible semi-automatic to automatic summary evaluation metrics will improve human-metric correlations compared to existing metrics.
The contextualization method improves over a non-contextualized baseline across all parts of speech.
An adaptation of the syntax-based model shows significant performance improvements in measuring text coherence.
Personalization methods will improve the effectiveness of predicting the perception of social norms in language processing tasks.

given_idea: The method produces highly reliable offensiveness scores.
